topic: Area B: Personal and Social Life
subject: spanish

items.q: How do you say your age in Spanish?
items.correct: With tener: 'Tengo quince años' - literally 'I have fifteen years'.
items.misconception: With ser, copying English: 'Soy quince años'.
items.why: Spanish treats age as something you have, not something you are; transferring the English 'to be' structure is a classic GCSE error.

items.correct: No - 'su' can mean his, her, its, their, or formal your; context decides.
items.why: One Spanish possessive covers several English ones, so learners must read the context instead of assuming a single fixed translation.

items.q: What does 'simpático' mean when describing a friend?
items.misconception: Sympathetic, because the words look alike.
items.why: 'Simpático' is a false friend: 'sympathetic/understanding' is 'comprensivo', and mixing them distorts personality descriptions.

items.q: How do you say 'I am fifteen years old' in Spanish?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Tengo quince años. “Soy quince años” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: 'Su hermana' can mean which of the following?
items.why: It's His, her or their sister. “Only 'his sister'” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence shows correct adjective agreement?
items.why: It's Mi madre es simpática. “Mi madre es simpático” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'El dormitorio' is which room of the house?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The bedroom. “The kitchen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
